paragraphs.body: My first impression of Schenley Park was of a courtyard rather than a park. Maybe it's because the name reminds me of a garden full of statues and neatly trimmed plants. The word “Shenley” reminds me of the English park with cobblestone paths, elegant benches and a peaceful atmosphere. The idea of an English park comes from the scene in Before Sunset where Jesse and Céline are walking along the Seine River. They walk along picturesque paths and discuss philosophy and poetry in the shade of old trees. Because of these preconceived notions, Schenley Park was destined in my imagination to be an oasis of serenity and well-maintained sophistication. But the truth is that I have visited Schenley Park three times, and each time a new perspective continues to challenge my initial impressions.

annotations.comment: Please use the past tense consistently throughout the paragraph.

paragraphs.body: First Glance The first time I visited Schenley Park was at the end of the summer, as a class trip for my second film class, and I was led to Schenley Park for the first time by my professor; it's worth noting that I'd already seen descriptions of the park on social media beforehand, so its name wasn't unfamiliar to me. But I was still blown away by what I saw; instead of the manicured formal gardens I had imagined, what I saw was a vast expanse of green space with a wild charm. The park was not sculpted and arranged as I had imagined; rather, it was a vibrant, gigantic creature settled in the middle of the city, giving me a glimpse of its wildness and vitality. The bridges over the lake were showing signs of rust from not being maintained for so long, and the paths were lined with weeds. The characteristic summer grasses and reeds took up almost the entire surface of the water, leaving only a few gaps that allowed me to see the plants under the water through the clear lake water.

Schenley Park presents a natural, untouched beauty. There are no stone bridges or pagodas to frame the landscape, and the trails are not carefully designed. The absence of fitness equipment or vendors is also striking - and these elements are common in Chinese parks. Instead, Schenley Park feels closer to nature's pristine state, with little human intervention. This return to the beauty of the natural environment balances nature and the urban environment. I began to understand that my initial assumptions about Schenley Park stemmed from unfamiliarity with American park culture and the influence of media portrayals. I had unconsciously projected elements from movies, television, and my cultural background onto my expectations of Schenley Park.

The rainy season in Schenley Park always begins with the spread of mold on the cast iron benches. On my second visit it was late fall, and since the semester was coming to an end, I decided to return to the area to find inspiration for my final piece for my film class. I crouched on the lakeshore to count the remains of algae, the dark green flotsam sprawled on the old steps like the green mold that always emanated from the damp walls of my old hometown of Anhui in the winter. Rotten acorns groaned sticky on the soles of my canvas shoes as I walked through the undergrowth. The fishy smell of wild mint mixed with the backwash of humus burrowed into my nostrils, reminding me of the never-drying school uniforms in my rented room during Shanghai's rainy season. The boxwoods, which should be pruned into globes, have grown into tumor-like monstrosities here, and the convenience store plastic bags hanging between their branches drip with last night's rain. Late fall twilight comes like instant coffee that has been watered down too much, and you have to taste it hard to enjoy the layers of bitterness. I sit on a rocky shoreline and nibble on a cold sandwich, watching a flock of mallards skim the railroad tracks and cast their reflections on the lake. As their flippers cut through the oil, the water rippled with the colorful ripples characteristic of the Suzhou Moat. Pittsburgh has cooled in the fall, and the jumper and short sleeves I wore were slightly thin in the windy weather. I was drawn to the cold, moist air I inhaled and completely succumbed to that environment. As I strolled along the riverbank, I felt a sense of serenity. There was only the gentle ripples on the water and the fallen leaves around me. I've always loved the trees in the fall when they shed their leaves in the twilight between life and death - a bare trunk that seems unlikely to flourish again in the spring. Their shedding, however, is the loveliest of moments: the leaves flutter freely from the branches, landing gently on the grass or, on windy days, swirling through the air as if forming a shimmering bonfire of rust and gold. As my shooting progressed, the sky darkened. The northern city of Pittsburgh is different from Shanghai, the city I grew up in. 上海的冷，不同于北方刺骨的寒冷。开始像秋风一样飒 爽，但等人察觉到时，就像蛆虫般附着在皮肤上，钻进骨头里了。 慢慢的，人就被浸 在上海的冷里了。 Pittsburgh, on the other hand, is cold in the north, and as long as one is wearing a good thick coat, even the strongest winds won't be particularly cold. But maybe it was because I was shooting by the lake, the damp and chilly air still soaked into my jacket- After the presentation about Schenley two weeks ago, I felt the need to go to Schenley again. Sometimes people have a finite relationship with many things, seeing them once for less, not knowing when it will be the last time. In Schenley someone had left torn canvas shoes with snow piled up on the insoles under the willow tree. The day I was there coincided with when the snow had just started to fall, and halfway down the road on the way there the sky started to flake. Snow varies from place to place; the snow in Pittsburgh was a short-lived snowfall that stopped falling for a few days in a row after that, leaving only fresh powdery snow that slowly turned to ice and then slowly melted to reveal the sun and the lawn. Pittsburgh's snow comes lightly, like the sound of thin paper being torn away, drifting down slightly, unhurriedly. Snow particles swirl in the air, knocking gently on the brims of hats and between brows, turning into glowing embers that linger for half a second in the sun's glow. Along the familiar path into the depths of the park, I saw a shallow layer of ice on the far side of the lake. The water plants that occupied the entire surface of the lake in the summer were no longer visible on the ice, and only the silhouettes of skaters crossed a white light from time to time. It was early winter in Schenley, and the thin ice made the surface of the lake seem to have a protective film. The snow fell quickly that day, so I went home soon after.

When I went the next day the ice was already thick there were people in thick down jackets standing around the lake in twos and threes talking and laughing. There were people clumsily stumbling around on the ice on skates, their laughter crisp in the cold air. I was a little surprised to learn that it's hard to find so many people playing in Chinese parks in the winter. At least in Shanghai, the willows in the parks are leafless in winter, their branches swaying slightly in the wind. On winter mornings you can only see old people practicing in the park, they may be playing tai chi or taking a walk, but it's rare to see so many young people playing sports in Schenley Park in winter in China. Watching them play hockey in the snow and ice gave me a little bit of joy, because when I was a kid I used to want to play in the snow too.

annotations.comment: Since this is a personal narrative, I would suggest using the first-person perspective consistently throughout the paper.

paragraphs.body: I've always loved snow, but it doesn't snow much in Shanghai. My elders said that Shanghai had an unprecedented snowfall in 2008, but unfortunately I hadn't started to remember yet. The biggest snowfall I remember in Shanghai was in 2018, when snow flakes were flying all over the place and a thin layer of snow could still be seen in the grass by the roadside. It was the biggest and only snowfall I had ever seen before I started traveling around after high school, because other than that all the other snowfalls I remembered in Shanghai just melted into water when they hit the ground. A former friend told me that people will spend a long time chasing things that were lacking when they were children, and that's probably how I felt about the snow, so at the time when I was choosing a college I decided to choose the western United States, where the seasons are so distinct. I picked up a dried leaf from the ground, which was covered with a thin layer of snow, and the veins of the leaf seemed to become clearer even in the cold.

Walking to the lake, I looked at the shadows skating on the ice and thought of the rivers in Shanghai. There are two rivers in Shanghai, one is the Huangpu River and the other is the Suzhou River. Huangpu River is wide and quiet, there are always freighters sailing slowly past, with a busy rhythm of the metropolis. Suzhou River is meandering, more like an introverted Shanghai old man. There are occasional floating leaves on the surface of the river, and many old memories are hidden in the shimmering waves. Shanghai's rivers, especially the Suzhou River, are always wet and gentle. Even in the coldest winter, the river surface will not freeze. The water gently laps against the stone embankment, bringing a hint of dampness and coolness, but it never stands still in a frozen state. In my impression of Suzhou Creek, the stone slabs on both sides of the river are always slightly wet and slippery, with water vapor floating in the air. In contrast, the thin ice on the surface of the lake in Schenley Park in front of me is less damp and gentle, but more frozen and quiet, just like Pittsburgh's former steel forest. That kind of quiet and Shanghai's long and soft are two very different landscapes. On Schenley's lake, I could feel the power of the northern winter. As I stood by the lake, I remembered the lecture I gave about the history and changes of this park, and the once bustling crowds and past times seemed to be frozen in the air at the moment. Every cold winter, Schenley's lake would freeze over like it did today, and then the children of the past would live with nature on this lake just like the people in front of me. The knowledge I have of Schenley that stays in the screen, like the frozen lake, slowly builds up until it melts away. My experience at Schenley Park made me realize the importance of assimilating into a new cultural environment without being bound by preconceived notions. I fell in love with the process of exploring cultural differences and it ultimately allowed me to appreciate the unfettered natural beauty of Schenley Park. I've always enjoyed direct contact with nature -hiking, climbing, and exploring the outdoors. This may explain why the park's lake area resonated with me from my first visit. Finally, my experience at Schenley Park made me realize the importance of exploring unfamiliar territory, both literally and culturally. While the cultural differences were initially a bit disorienting, they also allowed me to discover delightful aspects of American park culture. This process of learning and adapting broadened my understanding of cultural diversity. and helped me understand the deeper connections between people, places, and cultural identities.
